name: Tierra de Ágreda Loop via Cigudosa and Añamaza River Valley
name_original: Tierra de Ágreda — circular por Cigudosa y el Valle del Río Añamaza
description: This difficult 46.8-mile road cycling route explores the Tierra de Ágreda, offering scenic views of Cigudosa and the Añamaza River Valley.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Matalebreras are situated in the Soria province of Spain, offering diverse terrain for cyclists. The region is characterized by its position within the Moncayo area and at the foot of the Sierra del Madero mountains, providing varied topography with climbs and descents. Historical elements, such as remnants of a Roman road, add to the landscape. The natural environment includes holm oaks, savin junipers, and rockroses, contributing to the scenic backdrop for road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Matalebreras The most popular road cycling route is Magaña Castle and Alhama Valley Biosphere Loop, a 41.7 miles (67.1 km) difficult trail that takes about 3 hours 29 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain and passes by historical sites. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Cigudosa and Aguilar del Río Alhama Circular, a moderate 21.1 miles (34.0 km) path. This route offers a balanced ride through the river valley scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Tierra de Ágreda Loop via Cigudosa and Añamaza River Valley, a 46.8 miles (75.3 km) trail leading through the Moncayo region, often completed in about 3 hours 38 minutes. Road cycling around Matalebreras is defined by varied topography, including mountain foothills and river valleys, with routes that incorporate historical features like sections of Roman roads. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 90 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Matalebreras's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Matalebreras?
A: There are 9 road cycling routes around Matalebreras, offering diverse terrain for cyclists of various skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes near Matalebreras?
A: The region, nestled in the Moncayo area and at the foot of the Sierra del Madero mountains, offers varied topography. You'll find a mix of climbs and descents, with routes incorporating river valleys and historical features like sections of Roman roads. The Soria province is known for its attractive landscapes, providing scenic backdrops for your rides.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly road cycling routes in the Matalebreras area?
A: While many routes in the area are challenging, there are 2 moderate routes available. For a balanced ride through river valley scenery, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1383520369/cigudosa-and-aguilar-del-rio-alhama-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cigudosa and Aguilar del Río Alhama Circular&lt;/a&gt;, which is 21.1 miles (34.0 km) long.

Q: What are some of the more challenging road cycling routes around Matalebreras?
A: For experienced cyclists, there are 7 difficult routes. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1077372286/magana-castle-and-alhama-valley-biosphere-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Magaña Castle and Alhama Valley Biosphere Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 41.7 miles (67.1 km) trail with significant elevation gain that passes by historical sites. Another challenging route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1075667186/tierra-de-agreda-loop-via-cigudosa-and-anamaza-river-valley" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tierra de Ágreda Loop via Cigudosa and Añamaza River Valley&lt;/a&gt;, which is 46.8 miles (75.3 km) long and leads through the Moncayo region.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the routes are circular. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1383520369/cigudosa-and-aguilar-del-rio-alhama-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cigudosa and Aguilar del Río Alhama Circular&lt;/a&gt; offers a loop through river valley scenery. Another option is the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1075617240/palacio-del-papa-luna-by-the-sierra-de-moncayo-circular-from-olvega" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palacio del Papa Luna by the Sierra de Moncayo — circular from Ólvega&lt;/a&gt;, a much longer route for experienced riders.

Q: What historical landmarks or attractions can I see while road cycling near Matalebreras?
A: The region is rich in history. You can cycle past the Gothic church of San Pedro Apóstol in Matalebreras itself, or remnants of a Mozarabic tower. A section of the Roman road XXVII passed through Matalebreras, and a recreated section exists. Further afield, you might encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2718376" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of la Nava del Marqués de Magaña&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2683495" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of San Miguel Arcángel of Ágreda&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any viewpoints or natural features to look out for on the routes?
A: Absolutely. The routes offer views of the Moncayo region and the Sierra del Madero mountains. The natural flora includes holm oaks, savin junipers, junipers, rockroses, thyme, and chamomile, adding to the scenic beauty. You'll also find unique local art, such as a stone pyramid dedicated to Celtiberia and various iron sculptures depicting traditional trades throughout the village of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2718312" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Matalebreras&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in Matalebreras?
A: The Soria province generally offers pleasant conditions for cycling during spring and autumn, when temperatures are milder. Summer can be warm, while winter might bring colder weather, especially in the mountain foothills. Always check local weather forecasts before heading out.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling options around Matalebreras?
A: While the guide primarily features moderate to difficult routes, the diverse terrain means that some sections of the moderate routes might be suitable for families with older, more experienced children. It's always best to review the route details, including elevation profiles, to ensure it matches your family's ability.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling trails in Matalebreras?
A: Matalebreras is a small village, and parking is generally available within the village itself, particularly near its historical center. For routes starting from nearby towns like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6344175" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ólvega&lt;/a&gt;, designated parking areas are usually available in the town centers.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Matalebreras?
A: The komoot community frequently praises the varied topography, which includes both mountain foothills and river valleys, and the incorporation of historical features like sections of Roman roads. Cyclists appreciate the blend of challenging rides and scenic beauty that the region offers.
tips: This small town in the Tierras Altas region is located in the northeast of the province, its term is bathed by the Alhama river, which in turn is a tributary of the Ebro. When you pass the road that comes from Castelruiz, you can see the town of cigudosa

Cervera del Río Alhama is located on the banks of Cabretón, Aguilar del Río Alhama, Valverde, Fitero, Cintruénigo, on the banks of the Alhama River. It is divided into two neighborhoods by "La Peña", at the top of which are the ruins of the castle.
Ágreda gives its name to what is called Tierra de Ágreda and which represent the set of populations that are found together in the valley where the Río Añamaza passes. Nice town to take a break.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is rated as a "Hard road bike ride" and requires "very good fitness." With approximately 75 kilometers and 1,000 meters of elevation gain, it is best suited for experienced cyclists.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route features a mainly well-paved surface, making it suitable for road cycling. However, be prepared for potentially intricate and abrupt sections, and minor imperfections in the road surface, as is common in the region.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery will I see along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience diverse and picturesque scenery, including views of historic towns like Ágreda, Cervera del Río Alhama, and Cigudosa. The route also offers stunning panoramas of the Añamaza River Valley with its cliffs, rock formations, and waterfalls, as well as distant views of Moncayo.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Spring is highly recommended for cycling this route, as the flora is in bloom, filling the hillsides with vibrant colors and aromas. The weather conditions are also generally more favorable during this season.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the exact starting point for parking isn't specified, as a circular route, you can typically find parking in one of the towns along the way, such as Cigudosa or Ágreda, to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Are there any notable towns or landmarks along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through or near several points of interest. You'll cycle by the charming town of Cigudosa, Cervera del Río Alhama with its castle ruins, and Ágreda. The Añamaza River Valley itself is a natural highlight, known for its geological formations and potential to see the Cueva de los Murciélagos (Bat Cave).

faqs.question: Is this route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This is a road cycling route, and while dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, it's not typically suitable for cycling with dogs due to the distance, speed, and potential traffic. It's best to leave your canine companions at home for this particular adventure.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: No, this road cycling route does not require any specific permits or entrance fees. You are free to enjoy the public roads and natural landscapes.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several towns, including Cigudosa, Cervera del Río Alhama, and Ágreda. These settlements are likely to offer options for cafes, bars, or restaurants where you can refuel and rest.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other paths. You will find it intersects with parts of the Camino verde del Alhama, Camino Jacobeo Castellano-Aragonés: variantes, and Camino Natural del Agua Soriano. Camino Antonino, among others.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this road cycling trip?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, you should bring plenty of water, energy snacks, a repair kit for your bike, appropriate cycling attire for varying weather, sun protection, and a fully charged phone. A GPS device with the route loaded is also highly recommended.
